modules.title: The 5Ws + How
modules.subtitle: What GitHub is, who it's for, and why you're suddenly using it.
modules.intro:
Let's start where most tutorials skip past. **GitHub** is a website that holds folders of code — and the history of every change ever made to those folders. When you build something with **Claude Code** and run `git push`, your work lives in one of those folders, in the cloud, with a public link.

This module is the lay of the land. The other nine drill in.

sections.body:
Imagine a Google Doc, but for folders of code. That's the elevator pitch — and it's almost right. The two big differences:

**1) Time travel is built in.** Every saved version is recorded. You can rewind a file (or the whole project) to any point in its history. No 'final_v2_FINAL.docx' chaos.

**2) Many people can edit the same project safely.** Two collaborators don't overwrite each other — they each work on a copy, then *propose* their changes. Owner reviews, then merges.

sections.title: 5Ws + How
sections.body:
**Who** — anyone who writes code, plus more and more people who don't (designers, writers, hobbyists).

**What** — a website hosting `git` repositories, plus tools (Issues, PRs, Pages, Actions) wrapped around them.

**Where** — github.com (the website) and `~/Desktop/Projects/your-app` (a copy on your laptop).

**When** — every time you `git push`, GitHub gets the new version.

**Why** — backup, sharing, history, collaboration, free hosting (Pages).

**How** — `git` is the engine, GitHub is the website. Claude Code drives `git` for you.

If you've made anything with Claude Code, you already use this without thinking — you said 'push it to GitHub' and it just worked. This site is here to fill in the picture so the next time something's weird (`detached HEAD`, merge conflicts, a stuck `gh auth`), you know what story you're inside.

lines: $ git init                           # turn this folder into a repo
$ git commit -m "Initial commit"   # save a snapshot
# ↑ creates the repo on GitHub and pushes — done.

sections.body: Try the lightning quiz below. Six questions, answer with your gut — getting them wrong is the point, the explanations are where the learning happens.

modules.title: Anatomy of a Repository
modules.subtitle: What's actually inside a 'repo'.
modules.intro: A **repository** (repo) is a folder. The folder happens to be tracked by `git`, which means it has a hidden `.git/` subfolder full of bookkeeping. Everything else is just files. Once you see the cross-section, you'll never panic at `README.md`, `LICENSE`, or `.gitignore` again.

sections.body:
Hover (or tap on mobile) the labelled parts. The bits worth memorizing:

**`README.md`** — the front door. The first thing people read on github.com.

**`LICENSE`** — what others are allowed to do with your code. No license means 'all rights reserved' by default. Use **MIT** if you want people to use it freely.

**`.gitignore`** — files `git` should pretend don't exist. `node_modules/`, `.env`, `dist/` always go here.

**`.git/`** — the magic folder. Don't touch it. Holds every snapshot ever taken.

sections.title: Mental model
sections.body: Repo = a folder + a time machine + a front-door page. That's it.
When Claude Code creates a project, it scaffolds these files for you. Now you know which file is doing what.

modules.title: Finding Repositories
modules.subtitle: Treasure hunting on github.com.
modules.intro: GitHub hosts ~400 million repos. Most of them are someone's homework. The good ones — the libraries you'll actually use, the projects worth studying — share a few tells. This module teaches you to read those tells in a few seconds.

sections.body:
Three things I look at, in order:

**1) Stars** — like Twitter likes, but slower. 1k+ usually means 'this is real'. 10k+ means 'industry standard'. Less than 100 stars isn't bad — it means 'small or new'. Stars are a signal, not a verdict.

**2) Last commit date** — top-right of the file list. If the most recent change is from 2019, the project is probably abandoned. 'Updated last week' is the green flag.

**3) Issues / PRs activity** — the tabs near the top. Lots of *open* issues with recent comments = active community. Hundreds of *closed* PRs = maintainers who actually merge things.

sections.title: Don't fall for
sections.body: Polished READMEs alone. Slick logos and screenshots can hide unmaintained code. Always check the dates.

Search bar tricks worth knowing:

- `language:python` — only Python repos
- `stars:>1000` — only popular ones
- `pushed:>2025-01-01` — only ones updated this year
- combine: `claude code language:typescript stars:>100`

GitHub Trending (`github.com/trending`) shows what's hot right now — language-filterable, daily/weekly/monthly. Excellent way to discover what people are excited about.

lines: $ gh repo view anthropics/claude-code --web   # opens in browser

sections.body: Now play **Repo Detective** below — five real repos, one clue card each. Guess what each one does before peeking at the answer.

modules.title: Reading a README
modules.subtitle: A README is a contract. Here's how to read it in 30 seconds.
modules.intro: A good `README.md` answers four questions in the first screenful: **what is this**, **how do I install it**, **how do I use it**, **why should I care**. Once you know the standard sections, you can scan an unfamiliar repo at a glance.

sections.body:
The seven sections you'll see again and again, in roughly this order:

1. **Title + tagline** — what it is, in one line.
2. **Hero / demo** — animated GIF, screenshot, or live link.
3. **Features** — bullet list of what's included.
4. **Install** — `npm install`, `pip install`, `brew install`. Commands you can copy.
5. **Usage** — minimal example showing the thing actually working.
6. **License** — MIT, Apache, GPL — what you're allowed to do.
7. **Contributing** — how outsiders can help (mostly: 'open a PR').

sections.title: Red flag
sections.body: No `Install` section. If they don't tell you how to run it, the project is probably half-built.
The drag-drop game below shows you a real anonymized README. Drag each label onto the block where it belongs. Aim for 7/7 the first time.

lines: $ cat README.md | head -20      # peek at top of any README
> Coding agent that understands your codebase.
claude   # interactive session in your terminal

modules.title: Cloning & Using
modules.subtitle: Bringing a repo onto your laptop and running it.
modules.intro: **Cloning** means downloading a copy of the repo (with full history) onto your machine. After that, the project is yours to read, run, edit, fork — within the rules of its license.

sections.body:
Two ways:

**`git clone <url>`** — the original. Works everywhere `git` is installed.

**`gh repo clone <owner>/<repo>`** — the modern one. Same result, less typing. Auto-uses your GitHub auth.

Both land you in a new folder named after the repo. From there you `cd` into it and follow the README.

lines: $ npm install         # or whatever the README says

sections.title: License literacy
sections.body:
**MIT** — do anything, just keep the copyright line. Most permissive.

**Apache 2.0** — like MIT, plus a patent grant.

**GPL** — if you redistribute, your changes must also be open-source.

**No license** — all rights reserved. You can read it; you can't legally reuse it.

Cloning is *read-mostly*. To save your changes back, you'd need either to be a collaborator on the original repo, or to **fork** it (make your own copy on github.com) and push to that. Forks are how almost all open-source contribution starts.

modules.title: GitHub × Claude Code
modules.subtitle: Your actual workflow, drawn out so you understand it.
modules.intro: This is the keystone module — the one that makes all the others click. When you ask Claude Code to 'push it to GitHub', what's *actually* happening? Five steps, three tools, one diagram you'll never forget.

sections.body:
The five-step pipeline:

**1) Code lives in your project folder** (`~/Desktop/Projects/my-app`). Claude Code edits files there.

**2) `git` watches the folder.** `git status` shows what changed. `git add .` says 'I want to save these changes'.

**3) `git commit -m "..."` saves a snapshot.** A commit is a labelled bookmark in time. Good labels matter — that's the Commit Translator game below.

**4) `git push` sends new commits to GitHub** over the network.

**5) `gh` is the GitHub CLI** — it lets you do GitHub-website-things from the terminal. Create repos, open PRs, view issues, all without leaving Claude Code.

sections.title: Three tools, one job
sections.body:
`git` = save points (local).
`github.com` = the website (cloud).
`gh` = the bridge that makes them friendly.

lines: $ gh auth login                      # open browser, sign in
$ gh repo view --web                 # open this repo in browser

sections.body: When Claude Code does its thing, it's running these same commands behind the curtain. If something fails (auth expired, conflicting changes, no remote), you'll see the error — and now you'll know which step it came from.

sections.title: What 'good commit message' really means
sections.body:
**Verb-first.** `Add` `Fix` `Update` `Remove` `Refactor`. Imperative, like you're giving an order to the codebase.

**Under 72 characters.** Long enough to explain, short enough to scan.

**Mention the thing.** `Fix login` ✓ — `Fix bug` ✗.

The Commit Translator below grades your messages against this rubric.

modules.title: Branches & Pull Requests
modules.subtitle: Working in parallel without stepping on each other.
modules.intro: A **branch** is a parallel timeline. You start one, make changes, and the main project keeps going untouched. When you're happy, you open a **Pull Request (PR / 合并请求)** — a formal proposal: 'please merge my branch into main'.

sections.body:
The flow, every time:

1. `git checkout -b feature/login` — make a branch, switch to it.
2. Edit files. `git commit` your changes (could be many commits).
3. `git push -u origin feature/login` — push the branch to GitHub.
4. `gh pr create` — open a PR.
5. Reviewer leaves comments. You push more commits. The PR updates automatically.
6. Approval → 'Squash and merge' → branch is folded into `main`. Delete the branch.

sections.title: Why bother
sections.body: Branches let you *try* something risky without breaking `main`. If it works, merge. If it doesn't, throw the branch away. Zero lasting damage.

lines: $ # ... edit files, git add, git commit ...
$ gh pr create --fill   # uses your commit messages as the PR body

sections.body: **Local vs remote** confuses everyone at first. Local = your laptop. Remote = GitHub. `git commit` is local-only until you `git push`. Below — drag the actions into the right buckets to drill it in.

modules.title: Issues, Discussions, Projects
modules.subtitle: How GitHub becomes a project tracker.
modules.intro: An **Issue** is a thread for a single bug, feature request, or question. **Discussions** are like a forum. **Projects** are kanban boards. All three live next to your code, which is the whole point — context never leaves the room.

sections.body:
The trick to a good issue is the **template**. Even a casual one:

**Title** — verb-first, specific. `Fix dark-mode contrast on settings page` beats `Bug`.

**Steps to reproduce** — numbered, no skipped steps. The reader should be able to copy-paste them.

**Expected vs actual** — what you thought would happen, what actually happened.

**Environment** — OS, browser, Claude Code version. Saves a round-trip.

Try it in the converter below — paste a vague complaint, get a polished issue draft.

sections.title: Labels matter
sections.body: `bug` `enhancement` `good first issue` `help wanted`. They're how strangers find issues they can help with. Use them generously.

lines:
--body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Steps
1. Open settings page
2. Toggle dark mode
## Expected
High-contrast text
## Actual
Gray on dark gray, unreadable
EOF
)"

sections.body: Issues, PRs, and commits can reference each other: write `Closes #12` in a PR description and merging it auto-closes that issue. This is how a healthy repo stays organized — a web of cross-references that never goes stale because it lives next to the code.

modules.title: Pages & Actions intro
modules.subtitle: Free hosting + tiny robots that run on every push.
modules.intro: **GitHub Pages** turns a repo into a public website at `username.github.io/repo-name`. Free, fast, no servers. **GitHub Actions** are tiny robots that run on events ('on push', 'on PR opened') — they can lint your code, run tests, deploy your site, send a notification. Both are free for personal projects.

sections.body:
**GitHub Pages vs Cloudflare Pages** — both are free static hosting; both deploy on push.

- **GitHub Pages** is simplest if your project already lives on GitHub. Limit: 1 GB/site, soft 100 GB/month bandwidth.
- **Cloudflare Pages** has unlimited bandwidth, faster global CDN, native Workers integration, and previews-per-PR. It's a small extra step (add a project at `pages.cloudflare.com`) but worth it for anything you'd share publicly.

Most of the projects in this tutorial family deploy to Cloudflare Pages — `wrangler pages deploy dist`.

sections.title: When to use which
sections.body:
GitHub Pages — internal tools, docs, anything tiny.

Cloudflare Pages — anything you'll share publicly or that needs serious bandwidth.
Actions live in `.github/workflows/<name>.yml`. The simplest one runs on every push and reports whether the build still works. Toy example below:

Don't memorize the YAML. Recognize the shape: **name → on → jobs → steps**. When something fails on push, a red ✗ appears next to the commit on github.com — click it, you get the full log. That's enough to debug 90% of CI problems.

modules.title: Practice Lab
modules.subtitle: Five games, thirty flashcards, downloadable PDFs.
modules.intro: You've made it through the modules. The Lab is where you keep what you learned warm. Replay any game until it's automatic, flip flashcards on a coffee break, grab the briefing PDF if you want a paper copy.
